title: The Word and the Wreckage
bullets: "The Roaring Twenties" is a brand name applied after the fact, mostly in the 1930s, by people looking back at the years before the Great Depression with envy and a hangover.
Reconstruction collapsed in 1877 when a disputed 1876 election was settled by a bargain that withdrew the last federal troops, and the South was handed back to its white ruling class.
Jim Crow was not what the South always was but what a defeated experiment was replaced with by force: segregation, poll taxes, rigged literacy tests, sharecropping, and lynching.
The Supreme Court blessed it in 1896, ruling in Plessy v. Ferguson that "separate but equal" facilities were constitutional, making segregation the law of the land.

The Gilded Age built the railroads, steel, oil, the trusts, and vast immigrant labor; the Progressive Era then produced the income tax, the Federal Reserve in 1913, and Prohibition and woman suffrage.
Between 1880 and 1924 roughly 25 million people immigrated, the source shifting to southern and eastern Europe, the population the 1920s would slam the door on.
The 1920 census was the first to find more Americans living in cities than on farms, and the rural half experienced that as a loss of the nation it thought it had.
The United States entered the World War in April 1917, lost about 116,000 dead, became the world's leading creditor, and built a surveillance state it did not turn off.

title: The Plague and the Panic
bullets: The war ended at 11 a.m. on November 11, 1918, and Americans poured into crowds in the middle of the deadliest disease event in modern American history.
The 1918 influenza pandemic killed healthy young adults at high rates and killed roughly 675,000 Americans, more than died in combat in the World War, World War II, Korea, and Vietnam combined.
Philadelphia held a giant Liberty Loan parade on September 28, 1918, against public-health warnings, and within weeks the dead outran the supply of coffins.
The armistice cancelled war contracts overnight, prices jumped roughly 15 percent, and four million soldiers were mustered out with a small bonus and a train ticket and no plan.
1919 was the largest strike year in American history, with roughly four million workers, about one in five in the workforce, walking out at some point.
In February 1919 Seattle's entire union workforce of some 65,000 walked out in the first general strike in American history, disciplined and almost eerily peaceful.
When the Boston police struck in September 1919, Governor Calvin Coolidge sent in the State Guard and declared there was "no right to strike against the public safety by anybody, anywhere, any time."
In August 1920 the Nineteenth Amendment was ratified, enfranchising about 26 million women, though it did nothing about the race barriers blocking most Black women in the South.

title: The Fear
bullets: The November 1917 Bolshevik seizure of power in Russia made four million strikers and millions of immigrants look, to many Americans, like the beginning of revolution at home.

A small movement of Italian-American anarchists following Luigi Galleani really did mail roughly thirty bombs in 1919 and set off coordinated blasts, one wrecking Attorney General A. Mitchell Palmer's house.
The September 1920 Wall Street bombing killed about 38 people outside the J.P. Morgan bank, the deadliest such attack on American soil to that point, never solved.
The Palmer Raids of late 1919 and January 1920 arrested around 6,000 suspected radicals, mostly immigrants, mostly without warrants, with hundreds deported after administrative hearings.
The army transport Buford, the press's "Soviet Ark," sailed in December 1919 with about 250 deported radicals including the anarchist Emma Goldman.
The American Civil Liberties Union was founded in 1920, specifically in response to the sedition prosecutions and the raids.
Sacco and Vanzetti, arrested in May 1920, were tried before a judge who privately called them "anarchist bastards" and executed in 1927 after a trial the consensus holds was unfair.
The Red Summer of 1919 saw white mobs attack Black communities in two dozen cities, and on May 31–June 1, 1921 the Tulsa Race Massacre destroyed Greenwood, killing up to about 300.

title: Normalcy
bullets: In May 1920 Warren Harding promised "not heroics but healing, not nostrums but normalcy, not revolution but restoration," a fuzzy word for the promise to make the previous three years stop.
Harding won by the largest popular-vote landslide to that point, roughly 60 percent, about 16 million to 9 million, in the first election in which women voted nationwide.
Treasury Secretary Andrew Mellon cut the top income-tax rate from 73 percent to 24 percent over the decade, the supply-side argument in its first full American form.
Commerce Secretary Herbert Hoover turned a sleepy department into the most dynamic agency in government, promoting standardization, trade associations, and the regulation of the radio spectrum.
Harding's Ohio Gang cronies sold pardons, Prohibition immunity, and contracts, and Veterans' Bureau chief Charles Forbes looted that agency of an estimated $200 million.
In Teapot Dome, Interior Secretary Albert Fall secretly leased two naval oil reserves to private oilmen for about $400,000, becoming in 1929 the first cabinet officer imprisoned for crimes in office.
Harding said his "goddamned friends" kept him walking the floor at night, then died suddenly in San Francisco on August 2, 1923, before the worst scandals broke.
His death handed the presidency to the one man with no whiff of corruption, a silent Vermonter who learned he was president by lamplight at his father's farm.

title: The Silent Man
bullets: At 2:47 a.m. on August 3, 1923, by kerosene lamp in a Vermont farmhouse with no electricity, John Coolidge, a notary public, swore in his son Calvin as president, who then went back to bed.
"Silent Cal" understood that in a decade that wanted government to stop doing things and let the boom happen, the most popular thing a president could do was very little, very quietly.

His philosophy was compressed in January 1925 into "the business of America is business," a punchier misquote of "the chief business of the American people is business" that captured the policy exactly.
Coolidge twice vetoed the McNary–Haugen farm-relief bill on the grounds that propping up crop prices was not the government's business.
He ran in 1924 on "Keep Cool with Coolidge" and won comfortably while a Progressive third-party candidate, Robert La Follette, pulled nearly five million votes and carried only Wisconsin.
Between 1922 and 1929 GNP grew by something on the order of 40 percent, and for the first time the gains showed up as cars, radios, refrigerators, and movie tickets, much of it bought on credit.
The boom was real and narrow: farmers, the "sick industries" of coal and textiles, and Black Americans under Jim Crow mostly did not get it, with about a quarter of farmers losing their land.
On August 2, 1927 Coolidge handed reporters a slip reading "I do not choose to run for President in nineteen twenty eight" and walked away without questions.

title: The Machine That Made the Decade
bullets: Henry Ford did not invent the car or the assembly line but in 1913 at his Highland Park plant put the pieces together at a scale and effect nobody had managed before.
The moving line cut Model T chassis assembly from over twelve hours to roughly an hour and a half, and at peak a Ford plant finished a car about every half-minute.
The Model T's price fell year after year, bottoming under $300 in the mid-twenties, within reach of a skilled worker's roughly annual earnings rather than only a rich man's.
In 1914 Ford roughly doubled the basic wage to five dollars a day and cut the workday to eight hours, to stop the crippling turnover and so workers could buy the cars they built.
Alfred Sloan's General Motors refused to compete on price, offering "a car for every purse and purpose," the annual model change, and installment credit through GMAC.
By 1927 GM's strategy so decisively beat Ford's single cheap car that Ford shut his plants for roughly half a year to retool from the Model T to the Model A.
By the late twenties roughly one American family in two owned a car, a generation ahead of Britain, France, and Japan, where mass ownership waited until after World War II.
The car built the suburb, protected by zoning, plus the numbered-highway system and Route 66, and Muncie families in the 1929 Middletown study would "go without clothes" before the automobile.

title: Buy Now, Pay Later
bullets: In 1920 most American families could not pay cash for a car, refrigerator, radio, and washing machine at once; what squared the circle was the normalization of going into debt to buy things.
The installment plan dissolved the old moral stigma of consumer debt: take the thing home now, pay it off monthly with interest, the seller keeping the right to repossess.
By the late twenties somewhere between one in ten and one in seven of all retail sales, and most cars, radios, furniture, and appliances, were bought on installment.
Modern advertising, much of it on Madison Avenue, learned to sell not the product but relief from a fear or desire, as when Listerine pushed the word "halitosis" to sell mouthwash.
Bruce Barton's 1925 bestseller The Man Nobody Knows recast Jesus as a supreme advertising executive, an almost too-perfect confession of what was actually being preached.
The chain store, A&P and Woolworth and self-service Piggly Wiggly, undersold any single merchant and killed the general store's face-to-face credit economy.
Electrification went from a minority of homes to roughly two-thirds by the decade's end, but overwhelmingly urban, with the great majority of farms still having no power at all.
Labor-saving appliances did not save labor: advertising raised the standard, so the carpet beaten outdoors a few times a year was now vacuumed weekly.

title: The Broadcast Nation
bullets: On November 2, 1920 a Westinghouse engineer read election returns into a microphone over Pittsburgh station KDKA, conventionally counted as the first commercially licensed broadcast.
Radio did something never done before: it let millions of scattered strangers hear the same voice, music, joke, and advertisement at the same instant.
Britain built its BBC the same decade as a non-commercial public monopoly, the opposite institutional choice; the American advertiser model actively rewarded broadcast minstrelsy.
The Radio Act of 1927 created a Federal Radio Commission and the principle that the airwaves were public property, but NBC in 1926 and CBS in 1927 nationalized the content commercially.

The film industry consolidated into the studio system, many of its companies founded by Eastern European Jewish immigrants shut out of older industries, with weekly attendance in the tens of millions.
The lavish movie palace, the largest seating six thousand under marble and gilt, turned moviegoing into an opulent mass ritual.
Celebrity scandals like Fatty Arbuckle's 1921–22 trials and Rudolph Valentino's 1926 death frightened the studios into hiring Will Hays and writing the Production Code.
Warner Bros.'s October 1927 The Jazz Singer killed the silent era, its headline performance a white star, Al Jolson, in blackface, the same caricature radio's Amos 'n' Andy broadcast nationally.

title: The Door Closes
bullets: Congress slammed the immigration door in a four-year span, and it did so explicitly, openly, on stated grounds of race and national origin, with the science of the day in the committee reports.
The fuel was "100% Americanism," the postwar fusion of anti-immigrant, anti-Catholic, anti-Jewish, anti-radical, and anti-Black feeling into one movement of purity and grievance.
The Emergency Quota Act of 1921 capped immigration at 3 percent of each nationality already present at the 1910 census, favoring groups already numerous.
The Immigration Act of 1924, the Johnson–Reed Act, cut the figure to 2 percent and pegged the quota to the 1890 census, deliberately reaching back before the great new wave.
The act's "aliens ineligible to citizenship" clause barred essentially all immigration from Japan and Asia, extending the 1882 Chinese exclusion and poisoning U.S.–Japan relations for a generation.
The National Origins Formula took final effect in 1929, fixing total immigration at roughly 150,000 a year against the over 800,000 who had entered in 1921 alone, cutting the flow by four-fifths.
Eugenics was mainstream, taught in universities, and in 1927 the Supreme Court in Buck v. Bell upheld forced sterilization with Holmes writing "Three generations of imbeciles are enough."
The Indian Citizenship Act of 1924 finally granted citizenship to all Native Americans born in the country, though several states promptly found ways to keep them from voting.

title: The Invisible Empire
bullets: In August 1925 between 30,000 and 50,000 hooded men marched down Pennsylvania Avenue, and the men were as likely to be from Indiana, Oregon, or Colorado as from Georgia.
There were three different Klans; the first won its Reconstruction terror campaign and stood down, the third resisted civil rights, and this book's subject is the second.
The second Klan was refounded in a 1915 ceremony on Stone Mountain on ground prepared by the film The Birth of a Nation, then mass-marketed by promoters Edward Clarke and Elizabeth Tyler.
It presented itself not primarily as a lynching society but as a Protestant fraternal lodge, a temperance enforcer, and a defender of "old-stock" Americans, which made it socially respectable.
Peak membership estimates run from three to six million, meaning roughly one in fifteen or twenty native-born white Protestant adult men at the mid-decade peak.
Its violence was real: in 1921 at Mer Rouge, Louisiana, two men who spoke against the Klan were tortured and murdered, with no conviction because the Klan controlled the courts.
It controlled Indiana's government and shaped the 1924 Democratic convention, which took 103 ballots and defeated a plank merely naming and condemning the Klan by about one vote.
Indiana Grand Dragon D. C. Stephenson abducted and assaulted Madge Oberholtzer, was convicted of murder in November 1925, and the moral pretense and the whole movement collapsed within two years.

title: The Great Thirst
bullets: On January 17, 1920 the United States wrote a total nationwide ban on making, selling, and transporting alcohol into its Constitution, the only amendment that took a freedom away and the only one ever repealed.
Prohibition was the final victory of the Anti-Saloon League, the most effective single-issue pressure group to that point, which judged candidates solely on their wet or dry votes.
The Volstead Act set "intoxicating" at anything over one-half of one percent and funded enforcement so feebly that the federal force never topped a couple of thousand poorly paid agents.
Prohibition did reduce overall drinking, especially among the poor, but it drove the rest underground into speakeasies, of which New York had somewhere between 30,000 and 100,000.
The government deliberately poisoned industrial alcohol with methanol to deter bootleggers, knowingly presiding over the deaths of thousands of its own citizens.
The decisive consequence was organized crime: Prohibition handed local gangs a product with insatiable demand and colossal margins, financing the modern American crime syndicate.
Al Capone's Chicago Outfit grossed about $60 million a year, and the February 14, 1929 St. Valentine's Day Massacre, gunmen disguised as police, turned the public against him.
Capone was finally brought down in 1931 not for murder or bootlegging but for failing to file an income-tax return, and Prohibition was repealed in 1933 by the Twenty-first Amendment.

title: God and the Monkey
bullets: In July 1925 in eastern Tennessee a young schoolteacher was tried for teaching that humans evolved, the Scopes trial, carried live on the radio, settling legally almost nothing.
American Protestantism split between modernists, who reconciled faith with science, and fundamentalists, who insisted on the literal inerrant truth of the Bible.
"Fundamentalist" comes from The Fundamentals, cheap doctrinal tracts funded by two wealthy oilmen and mailed by the millions in the 1910s.
Tennessee's Butler Act of March 1925 made it a crime to teach "any theory that denies the story of the divine creation of man as taught in the Bible."
Dayton boosters recruited the substitute teacher John Scopes as a willing test case, drawing prosecutor William Jennings Bryan and defender Clarence Darrow.
When the judge barred scientific witnesses, Darrow called Bryan himself to the stand as a Bible expert and pushed him into confused, contradictory defense of biblical literalism.
H. L. Mencken's press wrote the trial as a modernist rout and a war on the "Bible Belt," and Bryan died in his sleep five days later, making the legend feel complete.

But the modernists won the press and lost the country: anti-evolution laws spread, textbooks muted Darwin for decades, and fundamentalism built its own parallel network including broadcaster Aimee Semple McPherson.

title: The New Negro and the New Sound
bullets: The most lasting thing the Roaring Twenties made was a body of music and writing created in upper Manhattan and the South Side of Chicago by exactly the Americans the decade's politics tried to suppress.
The Great Migration moved more than 1.5 million Black Americans out of the rural South between roughly 1916 and 1930, the largest internal migration in American history to that point.
It was Reconstruction's broken land promise finally cashing out, pushed by Jim Crow and pulled by wartime and post-1924 labor shortages and the smuggled Chicago Defender.
The Harlem Renaissance's manifesto was Alain Locke's 1925 anthology The New Negro, with Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, and Claude McKay's Red Summer poem "If We Must Die."
Marcus Garvey's UNIA built the largest Black mass movement in American history before he was convicted of mail fraud and deported.
The NAACP pushed an anti-lynching bill through the House in 1922 only to watch Southern senators kill it with a filibuster, a fifty-year-old Senate weapon.
In 1925 A. Philip Randolph founded the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters, the man whose threatened 1941 march on Washington would later force the first federal anti-discrimination order.

Louis Armstrong's Hot Five and Hot Seven recordings pulled the improvising soloist out front, and the Cotton Club presented Duke Ellington to a whites-only audience while performers entered through the back.

title: The Lost Generation and the New Woman
bullets: F. Scott Fitzgerald, famous at twenty-three, coined "the Jazz Age" and in 1925 published The Great Gatsby, which sold modestly then and became "the great American novel" only later.
The Lost Generation writers were the decade's internal dissent, saying in the country's best prose that the boom was spiritually empty while the advertising said it had never been happier.
"The Lost Generation" is Gertrude Stein's phrase, made famous by Ernest Hemingway's 1926 The Sun Also Rises, with many writers congregating in cheap, legal-wine Paris.
H. L. Mencken founded The American Mercury in 1924 and warred daily on Prohibition, the Klan, fundamentalism, and the "booboisie" that Sinclair Lewis had skewered in Babbitt.
The flapper, bobbed hair and knee hemlines and public cigarettes and the Charleston, was largely an urban, young, white, middle-class figure and substantially a media construction.
Women in the paid workforce were funneled into low-paid "pink-collar" jobs and mostly expected to quit at marriage; the vote had not delivered economic or legal equality.
The modern celebrity was manufactured at industrial scale: Babe Ruth's 60 home runs in 1927, and Gertrude Ederle, who in 1926 swam the English Channel nearly two hours faster than any man.
Charles Lindbergh's solo nonstop flight of about 3,600 miles from New York to Paris on May 20–21, 1927 drew a New York crowd estimated at around four million, fame itself as an industry.

title: The Bubble
bullets: The decade ended in a crowd of ordinary people who had borrowed against a future they were certain would arrive, discovering over about eleven days in October 1929 that it would not.
Florida's 1923–1925 real-estate mania, land flipped sight-unseen on a small binder payment, deflated in 1926 and was wrecked by a September 1926 hurricane that killed hundreds.
Herbert Hoover won a third consecutive Republican landslide in 1928 over the first Catholic nominee, Al Smith, declaring America nearer the final triumph over poverty than any land ever.
Buying on margin let an investor put down as little as ten percent and borrow the rest, with a margin call forcing immediate sale if the price fell.

Investment trusts owned shares in other companies on borrowed money, stacking leverage so a single dollar of value propped up many dollars of paper claims; broker loans hit about $8.5 billion by 1929.
Roger Babson predicted a crash and was mocked, and on October 17, 1929 Yale's Irving Fisher declared stock prices had reached "what looks like a permanently high plateau."
The Federal Reserve was paralyzed between popping the bubble and crushing the non-booming real economy, and dithered through 1928 and 1929 without acting in time.

The Dow peaked at 381 on September 3, 1929, then Black Thursday, Black Monday, and Black Tuesday's roughly 16 million shares evaporated tens of billions of dollars; the Crash triggered but did not alone cause the Great Depression.
